title: Expedition Member at High Camp — HACE
briefing: 52-year-old expedition member at 14,800 ft developed a severe headache and vomiting, now confused and unable to walk a straight line (ataxic). Classic High-Altitude Cerebral Edema. SpO2 critically low for the team. Descent is the treatment — do not wait for morning.
environment: High camp, 14,800 ft, cold and thin air
terrain: High alpine camp at 14,800 ft; descent on foot required, helicopter ceiling/weather dependent
evacNote: HACE — descend immediately even at night, assist/carry, oxygen if available

patient.mechanism: High-altitude cerebral edema (HACE) at 14,800 ft after ascent

injuries.notes: Ataxia, confusion, severe headache, vomiting. SpO2 78% at altitude. Decreasing level of consciousness — descent is definitive treatment.
